metadata.description: Defender for Endpoint baseline policy template
metadata.nistControls: SI-3 (malicious code protection), SI-4 (monitoring), SC-3 (security function isolation)
metadata.mitreAttack: Multiple — ASR rules map to specific techniques
metadata.author: JSA AppSec
metadata.deploymentNote: Deploy via Microsoft Intune, Group Policy, or PowerShell Set-MpPreference
metadata.WHY: Defender out-of-the-box is reactive. This policy makes it proactive: ASR rules stop common attack patterns before execution, cloud protection catches novel threats, and ASR exclusions are kept minimal to avoid bypass opportunities.

realTimeProtection.note: Never disable — this is the last line of defense

cloudDeliveredProtection.nist: SI-3 — cloud intelligence augments local signatures
cloudDeliveredProtection.note: High = faster response to novel threats; may increase false positives slightly

automaticSampleSubmission.note: Safe samples only — no PII or sensitive file content

tamperProtection.note: Prevents attackers from disabling Defender — critical for post-compromise persistence prevention

attackSurfaceReductionRules.WHY: ASR rules are the most impactful Defender feature for blocking initial access and privilege escalation. Each rule targets a specific attacker technique. Audit mode first, then Block after baseline.

rules.name: Block credential stealing from LSASS
rules.why: Mimikatz and similar tools dump credentials from LSASS. This rule blocks that read. The most impactful single ASR rule.

rules.name: Block Office applications from creating child processes
rules.why: Malicious macros spawn cmd.exe or PowerShell. This stops that execution chain. High impact on phishing attacks.

rules.name: Block execution of potentially obfuscated scripts
rules.why: Attackers obfuscate PowerShell to evade signature detection. This rule targets the obfuscation, not the signatures.

rules.name: Block untrusted/unsigned processes from USB
rules.why: USB-borne malware — classic air-gap attack vector. Block unsigned executables from removable media.

rules.name: Block JavaScript or VBScript from launching downloaded executable content
rules.why: Drive-by download pattern: malicious JS on web page triggers executable download. This breaks that chain.

rules.name: Block executable content from email client and webmail
rules.why: Attachments with executable content (.exe, .vbs, .js) directly from email clients. Blocks most phishing initial access.

rules.name: Block Office applications from creating executable content
rules.why: Macros should never write executables to disk. If they do, it's malicious.

rules.name: Block Office applications from injecting code into other processes
rules.why: Code injection from Office into other processes is an attacker technique, not a legitimate use case.

exclusions.WHY: Keep exclusions minimal. Every exclusion is a bypass opportunity. Document each one with justification and review annually.
exclusions.note: Do NOT add broad exclusions like C:\Users or entire application directories. Add only specific files or processes with documented business justification.

scheduledScanSettings.nist: SI-3 — regular scanning ensures detection of latent threats
scheduledScanSettings.note: Quick scan daily. Full scan weekly during maintenance window.

powerShellDeployment.description: Apply this policy via PowerShell (run as Administrator)
